The physician's admission order stating the expected length of stay; the patient's clinical presentation at admission showing the severity of illness (acute organ failure, sepsis criteria, major trauma, etc.); the H&P showing the conditions that required inpatient care; the physician's note documenting clinical judgment that 2+ midnights were expected; and any pre-admission testing results. Step 3 — In the appeal letter, cite the 2-Midnight Rule: 'Per CMS Chapter 6 of the Medicare Claims Processing Manual, an inpatient admission is appropriate when the physician expects the patient to require at least 2 midnights of medically necessary hospital care. At the time of admission, the patient presented with [CONDITIONS], which the physician reasonably expected would require 2+ midnights of care. This expectation is documented in the admission note.' Step 4 — For commercial payers: cite the specific InterQual or MCG criteria that the patient meets (e.g., 'For COPD exacerbation: respiratory rate > 30, O2 saturation < 90% on room air, pH < 7.35 or PCO2 > 50, which the patient met'). Include the clinical data in the appeal. Step 5 — Include the complete medical record: admission order, H&P, progress notes (first 2 days), nursing documentation (vital signs, assessments, IV records, respiratory therapy records), lab results, imaging reports, discharge summary. Documentation of physician involvement and daily monitoring is critical for inpatient admission appeals. Level of care appeals are high-stakes denials because they can result in significant payment reductions.

instruction: What is the appeal process for a Medicare Part B denial for Durable Medical Equipment (DME)?
output: Medicare Part B DME denials are managed by the DME MAC (Durable Medical Equipment Medicare Administrative Contractor). DME codes cover wheelchairs, walkers, hospital beds, oxygen equipment, CPAP machines, prosthetics, and orthotics. Common DME denial reasons: medical necessity (the equipment is not reasonable and necessary for the patient's condition); documentation missing (no physician order, no certificate of medical necessity); coverage criteria not met (the patient does not meet the specific coverage criteria for the DME item); supplier not enrolled; face-to-face requirement not met (for certain DME items, a face-to-face encounter with the ordering physician is required within 6 months before the order). Appeal process for DME denials: Level 1 — Redetermination: filed with the DME MAC within 120 days. Submit the written order, the CMN (Certificate of Medical Necessity, CMS-848), and medical records supporting medical necessity. Level 2 — QIC Reconsideration: filed with the QIC within 180 days. Submit all medical records. Level 3 — ALJ Hearing: AIC of $180+. DME appeals require specific documentation: the detailed written order (DWO — must include the item description, quantity, diagnosis, and physician signature and date); the CMN (DME-specific form for certain items); medical records documenting the patient's need (e.g., for a wheelchair: the patient's mobility limitation, ability to self-propel, home environment); face-to-face documentation (for items like power wheelchairs, the face-to-face note must be from a qualified practitioner). DME appeals are highly dependent on documentation — if the DWO is missing or the CMN is incomplete, the appeal will fail. A well-prepared DME appeal with complete documentation has a high success rate (60-70%).

instruction: How do you appeal a denial based on a missing or incorrect prior authorization when the auth was not entered into the system?
output: To appeal a denial for missing prior authorization when the auth was actually obtained but not entered into the billing system: Step 1 — Locate the original authorization. This requires searching for: email correspondence with the payer's authorization department; phone call logs showing the date, time, and representative who gave the auth (most phone systems record calls — request the recording); the auth number that was verbally given over the phone; the fax confirmation if the auth was faxed. Step 2 — If the auth was verbally obtained: request a copy of the phone recording or call log from the payer. Many payers maintain records of authorization calls for 2-3 years. Under ERISA, the provider has the right to obtain all documents relevant to the claim. Step 3 — If the auth number was not entered into the system at the time of claim submission: note that the claim was submitted without the auth number because the auth number was not available in the billing system (e.g., the auth was obtained by the clinical team but not entered into the billing system). The service was pre-authorized by the payer and should have been covered. Step 4 — In the appeal letter: 'We acknowledge that the authorization number was not entered on the claim due to an internal administrative error. However, the authorization was issued by your plan on [DATE] for [SERVICE] for patient [NAME], authorization number [AUTH#]. The service was provided as authorized. We are requesting that the denial be overturned and the claim reprocessed retroactively with the authorization number attached.' Step 5 — Even if the auth number cannot be located, some payers will honor a retroactive authorization review if the medical record shows the service was necessary and would have been authorized. Request a 'retroactive authorization review' as part of the appeal. Step 6 — For Medicare: Medicare does not require prior authorizations for most services (exceptions: DME, home health, certain inpatient stays). Missing auth is primarily a commercial payer issue. Prevention: implement a system where authorizations are entered into the PMS at the time of the auth call, not when the biller prepares the claim.

instruction: How do you calculate the ROI (Return on Investment) for an appeals program?
output: ROI for an appeals program measures the financial effectiveness of the appeal effort. Formula: ROI = (Total Revenue Recovered Through Appeals - Total Cost of Appeals Department) / Total Cost of Appeals Department x 100. Components: Total Revenue Recovered = sum of all overturned denials (payments received) + any additional payments from reprocessed claims. Total Cost of Appeals Department = salaries of appeals specialists (fully loaded with benefits) + cost of appeal submission systems (claims scrubber, denial management software, EDI costs) + cost of clinical documentation retrieval (HIM/medical records department time) + postage and mailing costs + external review fees (for IRO and other third-party reviews) + training and education costs. Example: a hospital system recovers $2,000,000 annually through appeals and spends $500,000 on the appeals department. ROI = ($2,000,000 - $500,000) / $500,000 x 100 = 300%. This means every $1 spent on appeals returns $3 in recovered revenue. Additional metrics: Cost per Appeal = total appeals department cost / number of appeals filed. Target: $25-50 per appeal. Cost per Successful Appeal = total appeals cost / number of successful appeals. Target: $50-100 per successful appeal. Recovery Rate = total recovered / total denied amount. Target: 20-40% (the appeals team should overturn 1 in 3 to 1 in 5 denials). A negative ROI indicates that the appeals program is costing more than it recovers — the provider should review appeal strategies and only appeal claims with a high probability of success. Most providers find an ROI of 200-500% for a well-managed appeals program.

instruction: What are the prompt-pay laws and how do they affect the appeals process?
output: Prompt-pay laws are state regulations that require health insurance plans to pay claims (or provide a denial determination) within a specific timeframe. Typical prompt-pay deadlines: electronic claims: 15-30 days; paper claims: 30-45 days; clean claims: 15-30 days; appealed claims: 30-60 days. If the payer fails to pay or deny within the deadline, many state prompt-pay laws require the payer to pay statutory interest on the claim amount (typically 12% per annum or 1-1.5% per month). States with strong prompt-pay laws: Texas (requires payment within 30 days for electronic claims, 45 days for paper), California (within 30 working days), New York (within 30 days), Florida (within 20 days), Illinois (within 30 days), Pennsylvania (within 45 days). How prompt-pay laws affect appeals: the appeal letter should cite the appropriate state prompt-pay law if the payer missed the initial payment deadline. For example: 'Per Texas Insurance Code Section 843.338, you were required to pay or deny this claim within 30 days of receipt. You have not responded within the required timeframe. We demand payment of the claim plus the statutory interest of 12% per annum.' Prompt-pay laws create leverage for the provider in appeals — payers are more likely to settle if they missed the deadline. Some states allow the provider to file a complaint with the state Department of Insurance for prompt-pay violations. The Department can impose fines on the payer. Prompt-pay laws vary significantly by state and apply only to state-regulated plans (not self-funded ERISA plans). ERISA plans are governed by federal ERISA regulations which have their own timing requirements.

instruction: How do you appeal a denial for a surgical procedure that was denied as 'bundled' with a diagnostic procedure?
output: To appeal a bundling denial (CARC 97 — 'The benefit for this service is included in the payment/allowance for another service'): Step 1 — Identify the specific NCCI edit pair. Check the current NCCI PTP file for the specific code pair. Note the modifier indicator (0 or 1). Step 2 — If the modifier indicator is 1: the edit CAN be bypassed with a modifier. The appeal should explain that a proper modifier was attached (or should have been attached). Provide the clinical rationale: 'The surgical procedure was performed at a separate anatomical site from the diagnostic procedure. The XS modifier (separate structure) was appended to the surgical code to indicate distinct services.' Step 3 — If the modifier indicator is 0 but the procedures were clinically distinct: the appeal must argue that the payer's automated NCCI edit was applied incorrectly because the two procedures were not inherently bundled. This is a harder argument but can succeed at higher appeal levels. Step 4 — If the denial is not NCCI-based but is based on the payer's own bundling policy: the appeal must address the specific policy language. Cite CPT Assistant or AHA Coding Clinic guidance showing that the two procedures are separately reportable. Step 5 — In the appeal letter: identify the specific edit pair; cite the modifier indicator and whether a modifier was used; explain the clinical distinctness (anatomic site, encounter, practitioner, or non-overlapping service); include the operative note showing the two procedures were performed separately; cite NCCI policy manual language supporting separate payment with a modifier. Step 6 — For payers that apply their own bundling edits (not CMS NCCI): check the payer's medical policy for the specific procedure. If the policy does not explicitly bundle the two codes, argue that there is no policy basis for the denial. A bundling denial appeal requires both clinical documentation and coding guideline citations.

instruction: What is a reconsideration vs redetermination in the Medicare appeals process?
output: In the Medicare appeals process, redetermination and reconsideration are two distinct levels: Level 1 — Redetermination: the first appeal level, conducted by the Medicare Administrative Contractor (MAC). The provider files a written request with the MAC within 120 days of the claim determination. The MAC reviews the claim, evidence, and supporting documentation and issues a decision. The MAC can affirm, reverse, or modify the original determination. Redeterminations are conducted by MAC employees (not independent contractors), and the provider can submit evidence that was not previously considered. Level 2 — Reconsideration: the second appeal level, conducted by a Qualified Independent Contractor (QIC) (an independent entity contracted by CMS, not the MAC). The provider must file within 180 days of the MAC's redetermination decision. The AIC threshold is $180 for Part B (2024). The QIC reviews the case de novo (from scratch), meaning they do not give deference to the MAC's decision. The QIC can reverse the MAC's decision. The QIC can request additional medical records or information from the provider. The QIC must issue a written decision within 60 days. Key differences: the MAC is the original Medicare contractor; the QIC is independent. The QIC can order the MAC to reprocess the claim differently. The QIC is the last level at which the provider can submit new evidence without restrictions (at the ALJ level, there are limits on new evidence submission). Many providers skip the QIC level if the dollar amount is small, but it is a required step before accessing the ALJ level (Level 3). The QIC reconsideration is often the most important level for high-dollar claims because the QIC is truly independent.

instruction: How do you appeal a denial based on 'unlisted procedure code requires documentation' (CARC 16)?
output: To appeal an unlisted procedure code denial: Step 1 — Understand the requirement. Unlisted procedure codes (e.g., 20999 for unlisted musculoskeletal procedure, 27199 for unlisted hip procedure) require supporting documentation to describe the service. CMS and most payers require: a description of the service; the operative report, medical records, and supporting documentation; a code that is comparable (a comparable CPT code for reference); and the rationale for using the unlisted code rather than a specific code. Step 2 — Gather the documentation: the operative report or procedure note describing the service in detail; any comparable code that is most similar to the procedure; the rationale why no specific CPT code exists for this procedure; pre-operative and post-operative diagnosis; any prior correspondence with the payer about the code. Step 3 — In the appeal letter: 'The claim was submitted with unlisted CPT code [XXXXX] because no specific CPT code exists for this procedure. The provider submitted the operative report and supporting documentation along with the claim. We are appealing the denial for incomplete documentation and request that the claim be reprocessed based on the submitted records.' Step 4 — If the payer denies the claim pending a medical review: request that the claim be referred to a medical reviewer (physician in the appropriate specialty). Unlisted codes require clinical review, not automated processing. Step 5 — If the payer denies because no comparable code was provided: provide a comparable code and a fee schedule reference. For example, 'This procedure is comparable to CPT [XXXXX] which has a Medicare fee of $XXX.XX. We are requesting payment comparable to that fee.' Step 6 — Prevention: always include the operative report with the initial submission when using an unlisted code. Use a comparator code that is most clinically similar. Some payers require a specific format for unlisted code submission. Check the payer's provider manual or companion guide.
